feed.title: Lourenço’s Blog
feed.subtitle: This is a simple blog to showcase projects I'm working/ have worked on and more.

entry.title: An intro to my blog
entry.content:
This blog will serve as a simple portfolio where I will be showcasing projects that I’m working and have worked on, be it of academic, professional or spontaneous nature.

Additionally, it will serve as an aiding tool to organize my thoughts for researching purposes - from summarizing papers pertaining to my investigational scope, to creating a taxonomic overview of research materials that I will come across with.

I hope you enjoy my journey through the fascinating world that Computer Science has to offer!

How marvelous it is to explore, in awe, the generous contributions that researchers have brought to the advancement of humanity, and how wonderful would it be to contribute to that cause…
entry.summary: This blog will serve as a simple portfolio where I will be showcasing projects that I’m working and have worked on, be it of academic, professional or spontaneous nature.

entry.title: An overview of Müller, Pascal, et al ‘Procedural Modeling of Buildings’ paper

link.title: An overview of Müller, Pascal, et al ‘Procedural Modeling of Buildings’ paper

entry.content:
Disclaimer:

This post serves as a mere summary for organizational purposes which will hopefully come in useful for the completion of my thesis’ report.

I will carefully attempt to appropriately cite and quote the authors’ work so that their intellectual property is preserved.

As a natural side effect of the paper’s publication date, the notion of time relative to that of grammatical conjugation within this overview might not old true to current standards.

Details regarding technically heavy terminology and implementation are excluded from this overview and will be reserved for future analysis.

Overview:

The Müller, Pascal, et al “Procedural Modeling of Buildings” paper presents a shape grammar, CGA Shape, for the procedural modeling of building shells and highlights important benefits which the entertainment and architectural domain can benefit from, namely low cost production and efficient generation of massive urban models with a high level-of-detail.

Additionally, it demonstrates solutions to previously unsolved modeling problems related to consistent mass modeling with volumetric shapes of arbitrary orientation and it is the first paper to address the aspect of volumetric mass modeling of buildings including the design of roofs.

Challenges that motivated this paper:

The authors identified a set of challenges that served as a motivational force to propose a solution:

  1. Modeling large 3D environments is both laborious and expensive
  2. Previous solutions were not able to generate sufficient geometric detail to buildings and were prone to unwanted intersections of architectural elements (Parish and Muller) (Show Fig. 2)
  3. Split rules are only sufficient for simple mass models, mass models can’t be easily changed, and objects of arbitrary orientation cannot be easily handled (Wonka et al)
  4. The derivation of shape grammars is intrinsically complex and usually done manually, or by computer, with a human deciding on the rules to apply Solution: Shape grammars can be simplified to set grammars to make them more amenable to computer implementation (Wonka et al)

Major contributions:

The Müller, Pascal, et al “Procedural Modeling of Buildings” paper constitute important contributions that are worth mentioning:

  1. A grammar-based solution, CGA Shape, which is an extension of set grammars introduced by Wonka et al capable of generating detailed building shells stemming from complex mass models
  2. A procedural approach to model detailed buildings with consistent mass models without being restricted to axis aligned shapes, including roof surfaces and rotated shapes
  3. Application related details in the context of procedural modeling of buildings - shape rules definition, concise notation, modeling strategies

An overview of CGA Shape:

On a general view, Müller, Pascal, et al’s CGA shape is powerful enough to specify complex shapes:

  1. Building mass models are constructed as a union of volumetric shapes
  2. Arbitrary rotations of shapes and cylinder inclusion in the shape vocabulary (show Fig. 6 as an example of rotations)
  3. Basic roof shapes inclusion - Gambrel, cone, gabled, hipped, cross-gable, mansard

As a result, CGA Shape is able to generate buildings with high visual fidelity by employing production rules that obey the following iterative process:

  1. Create the building’s volumetric model (mass model)
  2. Construct its façade
  3. Add details for windows, doors, and ornaments

This process constitutes a major benefit - the creation of an hierarchical structure that allows reusing production rules for procedural variations

Mass model construction:

The authors propose two possible ways to construct a mass model:

  1. Building lot as an axiom of the grammar
    • Generate mass models via scaling, translation, rotation, and split operations while being careful not to protrude parcel boundaries
  2. Importing from GIS database or architectural model
    • Tries to classify the imported model as basic shapes already defined in the shape vocabulary. If such an approach proves not to be possible, it uses general extruded footprint together with a general roof obtained by straight skeleton computation
    • Problem: Restrictive when it comes to modifications in the mass model (if any)

Façade construction:

With regards to directly computing visible façade surfaces, the authors enumerate the following problems (show Fig. 8):

  • Visible surfaces can be general polygons (not necessarily trivial to compute)
  • It is not clear how to write meaningful shape rules for general polygons
  • There is no simple mechanism to assign non-terminal symbols for the façade grammar, because these are mainly outputs of algorithms and not of production rules

As such, in order to overcome the aforementioned challenges, the authors proposed the following modeling strategy:

  1. Generate two-dimensional scopes aligned with façade surfaces and roof surfaces by extracting the faces of the 3D shapes with a component split Correctly aligned and parametrized resulting 2D scopes
  2. Extract edges by generating 1D scopes
  3. This works for arbitrarily aligned polygons such as roof surfaces and façade surfaces typically aligned with the up axis in the world coordinate system
  4. Resulting quads and triangles are then refined
  5. After a shape is reduced to 2D it is often replaced by 3D through subsequent rule applications Solution to consistent design: - Testing spatial overlap (occlusion) - Testing nearby important lines and planes in the shape configuration (snap lines) This model retains the simplicity necessary for procedural modeling while allowing to work many configurations of mass models.

Occlusion

  • Occlusion queries that test for intersections between shapes (no occlusion, partial occlusion, full occlusion)
  • Simplest query can check if a selected shape is occluded by any other shape
  • There are several variations to query subsets of a shape’s configuration
    • Occlusion against all shapes (even inactive ones)
    • Occlusion against shapes with a specific label
    • Occlusion against all shapes except the selected shape’s predecessors
  • Type of intersection computation can also be modified to include distance for analyzing occlusions when enlarging a model

Snapping

  • Occlusion makes it possible to avoid placing façade elements on the intersection of volumetric shapes constituting the mass model, but the structure of the façade’s layout can further be improved
  • Improvement - Alter existing shape rules to snap to a dominant face or line (snap shapes) in the shape configuration
  • These operations will be performed in 2D faces of the volumetric shapes
  • Snap lines work for repeat split and subdivision split: Repeat: - Snap lines divide the scope into different parts and the repeat rule is invoked for each part separately Subdivision: - Snap line just alters the closest split and leaves everything else unmodified

Discussion:

Comparison to mesh modeling tools:

  • Existing commercial scripting to accelerate modeling processes can only replicate parts of CGA Shape
  • CGA Shape models are significantly more detailed than modeling practices in the entertainment industry
  • While the global structure, the positioning of individual shapes, level-of-detail control, and the data handling of large models is a strength of CGA Shape, the generation of smaller complex geometric details is sometimes inefficient
  • CGA shape is a significant step forward that reduces modeling times by orders of magnitude.

Efficiency and robustness:

  • Complex and error-prone geometric operations do not have to be executed
  • Good tradeoff between visual quality and speed
  • Derivation of shape grammar reasonably fast (one billion polygon models can be generated in less than one day)
  • Disadvantage - Procedural approach sometimes generates configurations of shapes that aren’t plausible (Future research - Shape grammars for shape understanding)

Usability:

  • Learning curve similar to other scripting languages
  • Carelessly written rules can be very contrived and will only be understood by the original author and might produce unwanted side effects
  • Modeling with CGA Shape is most naturally understood by people with a computer science background

Architecture and computer graphics:

Application to architectural design is yet to be explored and might require significant (e.g. adapting architectural concepts and deriving a set of specific rules)

Comparison to L-Systems:

Similarities:

  • Notation of rules
  • Idea of scope -> Evolution of L-System turtle
  • Necessity of context sensitive rules

Distinctions:

  • Shapes, and rules replacing shapes with shapes instead of string replacement
  • Shape rules non-existing in L-Systems
  • L-Systems produces rules governing a biological system which do not directly relate to the modeling of buildings
  • Direct application of L-Systems to architecture emphasizes the idea of growth (counterproductive for procedural modeling of buildings)

Comparison to instant architecture:

Real-time rendering:

  • Requires post-processing algorithms not yet developed (Challenge - Develop LOD techniques for massive city models)
entry.summary: Disclaimer: This post serves as a mere summary for organizational purposes which will hopefully come in useful for the completion of my thesis’ report. I will carefully attempt to appropriately cite and quote the authors’ work so that their intellectual property is preserved. As a natural side effect of the paper’s publication date, the notion of time relative to that of grammatical conjugation within this overview might not old true to current standards. Details regarding technically heavy terminology and implementation are excluded from this overview and will be reserved for future analysis. Overview: The Müller, Pascal, et al “Procedural Modeling of Buildings” paper presents a shape grammar, CGA Shape, for the procedural modeling of building shells and highlights important benefits which the entertainment and architectural domain can benefit from, namely low cost production and efficient generation of massive urban models with a high level-of-detail. Additionally, it demonstrates solutions to previously unsolved modeling problems related to consistent mass modeling with volumetric shapes of arbitrary orientation and it is the first paper to address the aspect of volumetric mass modeling of buildings including the design of roofs. Challenges that motivated this paper: The authors identified a set of challenges that served as a motivational force to propose a solution: Modeling large 3D environments is both laborious and expensive Previous solutions were not able to generate sufficient geometric detail to buildings and were prone to unwanted intersections of architectural elements (Parish and Muller) (Show Fig. 2) Split rules are only sufficient for simple mass models, mass models can’t be easily changed, and objects of arbitrary orientation cannot be easily handled (Wonka et al) The derivation of shape grammars is intrinsically complex and usually done manually, or by computer, with a human deciding on the rules to apply Solution: Shape grammars can be simplified to set grammars to make them more amenable to computer implementation (Wonka et al) Major contributions: The Müller, Pascal, et al “Procedural Modeling of Buildings” paper constitute important contributions that are worth mentioning: A grammar-based solution, CGA Shape, which is an extension of set grammars introduced by Wonka et al capable of generating detailed building shells stemming from complex mass models A procedural approach to model detailed buildings with consistent mass models without being restricted to axis aligned shapes, including roof surfaces and rotated shapes Application related details in the context of procedural modeling of buildings - shape rules definition, concise notation, modeling strategies An overview of CGA Shape: On a general view, Müller, Pascal, et al’s CGA shape is powerful enough to specify complex shapes: Building mass models are constructed as a union of volumetric shapes Arbitrary rotations of shapes and cylinder inclusion in the shape vocabulary (show Fig. 6 as an example of rotations) Basic roof shapes inclusion - Gambrel, cone, gabled, hipped, cross-gable, mansard As a result, CGA Shape is able to generate buildings with high visual fidelity by employing production rules that obey the following iterative process: Create the building’s volumetric model (mass model) Construct its façade Add details for windows, doors, and ornaments This process constitutes a major benefit - the creation of an hierarchical structure that allows reusing production rules for procedural variations Mass model construction: The authors propose two possible ways to construct a mass model: Building lot as an axiom of the grammar Generate mass models via scaling, translation, rotation, and split operations while being careful not to protrude parcel boundaries Importing from GIS database or architectural model Tries to classify the imported model as basic shapes already defined in the shape vocabulary. If such an approach proves not to be possible, it uses general extruded footprint together with a general roof obtained by straight skeleton computation Problem: Restrictive when it comes to modifications in the mass model (if any) Façade construction: With regards to directly computing visible façade surfaces, the authors enumerate the following problems (show Fig. 8): Visible surfaces can be general polygons (not necessarily trivial to compute) It is not clear how to write meaningful shape rules for general polygons There is no simple mechanism to assign non-terminal symbols for the façade grammar, because these are mainly outputs of algorithms and not of production rules As such, in order to overcome the aforementioned challenges, the authors proposed the following modeling strategy: Generate two-dimensional scopes aligned with façade surfaces and roof surfaces by extracting the faces of the 3D shapes with a component split Correctly aligned and parametrized resulting 2D scopes Extract edges by generating 1D scopes This works for arbitrarily aligned polygons such as roof surfaces and façade surfaces typically aligned with the up axis in the world coordinate system Resulting quads and triangles are then refined After a shape is reduced to 2D it is often replaced by 3D through subsequent rule applications Solution to consistent design: - Testing spatial overlap (occlusion) - Testing nearby important lines and planes in the shape configuration (snap lines) This model retains the simplicity necessary for procedural modeling while allowing to work many configurations of mass models. Occlusion Occlusion queries that test for intersections between shapes (no occlusion, partial occlusion, full occlusion) Simplest query can check if a selected shape is occluded by any other shape There are several variations to query subsets of a shape’s configuration Occlusion against all shapes (even inactive ones) Occlusion against shapes with a specific label Occlusion against all shapes except the selected shape’s predecessors Type of intersection computation can also be modified to include distance for analyzing occlusions when enlarging a model Snapping Occlusion makes it possible to avoid placing façade elements on the intersection of volumetric shapes constituting the mass model, but the structure of the façade’s layout can further be improved Improvement - Alter existing shape rules to snap to a dominant face or line (snap shapes) in the shape configuration These operations will be performed in 2D faces of the volumetric shapes Snap lines work for repeat split and subdivision split: Repeat: - Snap lines divide the scope into different parts and the repeat rule is invoked for each part separately Subdivision: - Snap line just alters the closest split and leaves everything else unmodified Discussion: Comparison to mesh modeling tools: Existing commercial scripting to accelerate modeling processes can only replicate parts of CGA Shape CGA Shape models are significantly more detailed than modeling practices in the entertainment industry While the global structure, the positioning of individual shapes, level-of-detail control, and the data handling of large models is a strength of CGA Shape, the generation of smaller complex geometric details is sometimes inefficient CGA shape is a significant step forward that reduces modeling times by orders of magnitude. Efficiency and robustness: Complex and error-prone geometric operations do not have to be executed Good tradeoff between visual quality and speed Derivation of shape grammar reasonably fast (one billion polygon models can be generated in less than one day) Disadvantage - Procedural approach sometimes generates configurations of shapes that aren’t plausible (Future research - Shape grammars for shape understanding) Usability: Learning curve similar to other scripting languages Carelessly written rules can be very contrived and will only be understood by the original author and might produce unwanted side effects Modeling with CGA Shape is most naturally understood by people with a computer science background Architecture and computer graphics: Application to architectural design is yet to be explored and might require significant (e.g. adapting architectural concepts and deriving a set of specific rules) Comparison to L-Systems: Similarities: Notation of rules Idea of scope -> Evolution of L-System turtle Necessity of context sensitive rules Distinctions: Shapes, and rules replacing shapes with shapes instead of string replacement Shape rules non-existing in L-Systems L-Systems produces rules governing a biological system which do not directly relate to the modeling of buildings Direct application of L-Systems to architecture emphasizes the idea of growth (counterproductive for procedural modeling of buildings) Comparison to instant architecture: Real-time rendering: Requires post-processing algorithms not yet developed (Challenge - Develop LOD techniques for massive city models)

entry.title: A brief introduction to my thesis: Procedural Modeling of Architectonic Shapes

link.title: A brief introduction to my thesis: Procedural Modeling of Architectonic Shapes

entry.content:
My thesis aims at exploring and applying procedural modeling techniques for modeling both structural and ornamental assets inherent to that of the architectural domain.

Additionally, my thesis will also present a reflection concerning already existing solutions for procedural modeling techniques applied to architecture - their benefits and limitations - as well as its already proven potential as an indispensable tool for architects during an era where technological integration is of the essence - especially for the optimization and exploration of countless combinatorial variations pertaining to a determined set of outputs, otherwise unfeasible to inexpensively achieve by hand in a timely manner.
entry.summary: My thesis aims at exploring and applying procedural modeling techniques for modeling both structural and ornamental assets inherent to that of the architectural domain.
